The Bon tradition, while sharing many external similarities with Tibetan Buddhism, has its own distinctive canon of scriptures, pantheon of deities, meditation practices, and ritual traditions that trace their origins to the legendary figure of Tonpa Shenrab Miwoche. This monastery represents a unique and precious element of Sikkim's religious diversity, preserving Bon practices that exist alongside the Buddhist traditions of the region. The monastery is visited by practitioners and scholars interested in the Bon spiritual heritage, and its festivals — which follow the Bon religious calendar — offer a window into this ancient and distinctive Himalayan tradition.

name: Burmiok Norbugang Monastery
history: Burmiok Norbugang Monastery, founded in 1992, is a modern Nyingma monastery in the Burmiok area of South Sikkim, established as a companion institution to the older Burmiok Wosel Choling Monastery in the same village. The name 'Norbugang' means 'Heap of Precious Jewels,' evoking the image of the monastery as a treasury overflowing with the precious jewels of Buddhist wisdom, compassion, and spiritual attainment. Established in the post-integration era of Sikkim, the monastery was founded to serve the growing spiritual needs of a community that had expanded significantly in the decades following 1975. Burmiok Norbugang maintains a contemporary approach to Buddhist community engagement, combining traditional Nyingma ritual practice with modern educational programmes, youth outreach, and environmental activities inspired by the Buddhist principle of interdependence and care for all beings. The monastery has quickly become an important community centre in Burmiok.

name: Ralong Palchen Choling Monastery
history: Ralong Palchen Choling Monastery, established in 1995 as the New Ralang Monastery, is one of the most architecturally impressive and spiritually vibrant Buddhist institutions in South Sikkim, and indeed in all of Sikkim. The name 'Palchen Choling' means 'Great Glorious Dharma Place,' and the monastery is grand in every sense — its main temple rises four storeys, crowned with a gleaming golden roof, and houses a magnificent 35-foot statue of Guru Padmasambhava that is one of the tallest such statues in Sikkim. The monastery was established by the same Kagyu lineage that built the original Ralang Monastery in 1730, ensuring the continuation of nearly three centuries of Karma Kagyu tradition in the Ralong area. Ralong Palchen Choling is famous for its spectacular festivals — particularly the Pang Lhabsol celebration in which the sacred mountains of Sikkim are honoured as protector deities — and for its monastic college, where monks receive comprehensive training in Kagyu Buddhist philosophy and practice.

name: Namtse Nga-dag Monastery
history: Namtse Nga-dag Monastery, established in 1684, is one of the oldest monasteries in South Sikkim, founded during the reign of the early Chogyal kings as a centre of Nyingma Buddhist practice. The name 'Namtse Nga-dag' translates roughly as 'Lord of the Celestial Heights,' reflecting its elevated spiritual status in the community. Founded by lamas who travelled from Tibet carrying ancient scriptures, sacred relics, and the blessings of Nyingma lineage masters, the monastery has weathered over three centuries of political upheaval and natural calamity while remaining a steadfast beacon of Buddhist faith. Its prayer hall houses rare thangka paintings, hand-carved wooden altars, and centuries-old butter lamps. The monks are trained in classical Tibetan rituals including the sacred cham dances performed during Losar and Saga Dawa. The monastery is also a repository of ancient manuscripts, many dating to its founding era, and continues as a living centre of Nyingma spirituality.

name: Yangang Gonpa Tashi Palding Gon
history: Yangang Gonpa Tashi Palding Gon, founded in 1787 in the village of Yangang, South Sikkim, is a monastery of considerable historical and spiritual importance. The name 'Tashi Palding Gon' translates as 'The Monastery of Auspicious and Glorious Ground,' reflecting the sacred nature attributed to the site by its Nyingma founders. Built during a period of political consolidation in Sikkim, the monastery was quickly established as the spiritual heart of the Yangang community. The founding lamas performed elaborate consecration rituals, burying sacred treasures (terma) beneath the foundation stones for the monastery's spiritual protection. The interior assembly hall features remarkable frescoes depicting Guru Padmasambhava, Amitabha Buddha, and the Eight Manifestations of the Guru, painted in traditional mineral pigments that remain vivid to this day. Over the generations, the monastery has nurtured a lineage of accomplished monks, including several recognised as reincarnate lamas (tulkus). It remains deeply involved in the religious and cultural life of Yangang.

name: Mangbro Monastery
history: Mangbro Monastery, established in 1790, is a traditional Nyingma monastery in South Sikkim, founded in the late 18th century when Buddhism was firmly established as the state religion of Sikkim and monastic institutions enjoyed royal patronage. The founding Nyingma lama identified the site as geomantically auspicious — a place where natural elements were in perfect harmony for sustained meditation and spiritual practice. The name 'Mangbro' is associated with concepts of abundance and prosperity, reflecting the monastery's role as a source of spiritual wealth for the community. The main shrine houses a magnificent statue of Guru Padmasambhava flanked by his two consorts, Yeshe Tsogyal and Mandarava, considered the spiritual guardian of the community. Mangbro has been particularly noted as a centre for the practice of Dzogchen — the Great Perfection — a highly advanced meditation practice unique to the Nyingma school. Pilgrims continue to visit in large numbers during Losar, Saga Dawa, and Guru Rinpoche's anniversary.

name: Linge Phagyal Monastery
history: Linge Phagyal Monastery, founded in 1862 in South Sikkim, was established by a Nyingma lama who had received extensive training in Buddhist philosophy and ritual practice in Tibet before returning to Sikkim to establish a centre of learning and worship. The name 'Linge Phagyal' carries deep spiritual significance — 'Phagyal' refers to the victorious and exalted nature of the Buddhist teachings, suggesting a monastery that rises triumphant like a sacred peak. From its early years, the monastery distinguished itself as a centre for Buddhist education, offering training in philosophy, ritual performance, thangka painting, and traditional medicine. Its curriculum was modelled on the great monastic universities of Tibet. The monastery's inner walls are decorated with exquisite murals depicting the entire cosmology of the Buddhist universe, and among its most cherished possessions is a collection of sacred relics brought from Tibet by its founder, enshrined in a golden chorten at the heart of the main shrine room.

name: Burmiok Wosel Choling Monastery
history: Burmiok Wosel Choling Monastery, established in 1873, is a community-centred Nyingma monastery in Burmiok, South Sikkim, whose name 'Wosel Choling' means 'The Place of Clear Light Dharma' — reflecting dedication to the highest Nyingma teachings on luminous awareness. The founding lama was known for making profound Buddhist teachings accessible to ordinary farmers, traders, and families, not just ordained monks, giving the monastery a warm and inclusive character that has persisted to this day. The monastery was built on land donated by a prominent local family through community effort, and the main shrine houses elaborate altar arrangements with gilded statues of Amitabha Buddha, Guru Padmasambhava, and the Medicine Buddha. The monastery established a school for local children in its premises during the early 20th century and continues to support education and community development. Its annual cham festival is one of the most beloved cultural events in the Burmiok area.

name: Ben Karma Choling Monastery
history: Ben Karma Choling Monastery, founded in 1902 in the village of Ben, South Sikkim, is a notable Karma Kagyu monastery that was established at the turn of the 20th century as Sikkim was experiencing increasing British Indian influence. Despite external pressures, the monastic institution at Ben remained a steadfast guardian of Tibetan Buddhist culture. The name 'Karma Choling' — 'The Place of Dharma of the Karma Kagyu' — directly proclaims its lineage affiliation. The founding lama was a disciple of senior Kagyu masters who came to Ben with the desire to establish a permanent monastic community serving both ordained monks and lay practitioners. The monastery grew from a small prayer hall into a substantial complex with a main temple, monk's quarters, a library, and open courtyards. The monks are renowned throughout South Sikkim for the precision and beauty of their chanting and ceremonial performance. The monastery has also been instrumental in preserving traditional Tibetan arts including thangka painting and butter sculpture.

name: Namthang Norbu Tsho-ling Monastery
history: Namthang Norbu Tsho-ling Monastery, established in 1914 in Namthang, South Sikkim, is a spiritually luminous monastery whose name 'Norbu Tsho-ling' means 'The Jewel Lake Dharma Place,' evoking the boundless depth and preciousness of the Buddhist teachings. Founded in the early 20th century during a dynamic period in Sikkimese history, the founding lama's vision was of a monastery serving not only as a place of worship but as a comprehensive centre for Buddhist learning, arts, and social welfare. The monastery rapidly became an important institution in Namthang, its main shrine room centred around an impressive representation of Amitayus (the Buddha of Boundless Life). The walls are adorned with thangka paintings depicting the lives of the eighty-four Mahasiddhas, commissioned as inspiration for practitioners. Namthang Norbu Tsho-ling has maintained strong traditions of annual Dharma courses and meditation retreats, drawing practitioners from across Sikkim, and has also been active in social welfare, education, and disaster relief efforts over the decades.

name: Malli Tashi Chodarling Monastery
history: Malli Tashi Chodarling Monastery, founded in 1915 in the village of Malli, South Sikkim, is a cherished Nyingma monastery whose name 'Tashi Chodarling' means 'The Auspicious Place Where the Dharma Spreads.' The founding Nyingma lama identified the site at Malli as particularly sacred based on the presence of auspicious natural features — springs, old growth trees, and a specific configuration of hills associated with spiritual power in traditional Buddhist geomancy. The founding was marked by a grand ceremony attended by monks from across South Sikkim. The monastery is especially known for its annual Tsechu celebration on the tenth day of the Tibetan lunar month, drawing hundreds of devotees to watch spectacular cham performances. Its library holds a significant collection of canonical Buddhist texts including rare terma (treasure texts) discovered by Nyingma masters of previous centuries. Malli Tashi Chodarling continues as a living centre of Nyingma Buddhist practice, transmitting ancient wisdom to new generations.

name: Wok Pabong Monastery
history: Wok Pabong Monastery, established in 1915 in the village of Wok, South Sikkim, was founded by a Nyingma lama who came to the village following a prophetic vision in which he saw the site as designated by Guru Padmasambhava for the establishment of a monastery. The name 'Pabong' carries associations with a protective peak or fortress, suggesting the monastery's role as a spiritual stronghold for the Wok community. Its establishment transformed the religious and cultural landscape of Wok — for the first time, the village had a permanent monastic institution providing regular prayers, rituals, and teachings, allowing young men to receive monastic training locally. The monastery's main shrine features a central statue of Shakyamuni Buddha surrounded by the Seven Medicine Buddhas, reflecting the monastery's traditional role in offering prayers for community health and well-being. Over its century of existence, Wok Pabong has conducted tens of thousands of ceremonies on behalf of the living and deceased of its community.

name: Sangmo Sharchog Bephug Monastery
history: Sangmo Sharchog Bephug Monastery, founded in 1921 in the village of Sangmo, South Sikkim, is a monastery of unique spiritual character. Its name 'Sharchog Bephug' translates as 'The Eastern Cave of Perfection,' hinting at a tradition of cave meditation that preceded the formal monastery — local tradition holds that a wandering Nyingma yogi spent years meditating in a natural cave at this location, transforming the site into a place of powerful spiritual energy. The monastery is particularly renowned for its practice of Chöd — a distinctive Nyingma and Kagyu meditation practice in which the practitioner symbolically offers their own body to spirits as an act of supreme generosity and a means to overcome ego-clinging, performed using a hand drum (damaru) and curved knife (kartika) during the dark hours of the night. The monastery is considered one of the premier centres for Chöd practice in South Sikkim, and its annual festivals draw experienced practitioners from across the Himalayan region seeking this concentrated spiritual atmosphere.

name: Parbing Samten Choling Monastery
history: Parbing Samten Choling Monastery, established in 1935 in the village of Parbing, South Sikkim, is a deeply contemplative institution whose name 'Samten Choling' means 'The Place of Meditation and Dharma.' Founded by a Nyingma master who had completed multiple three-year retreats, the monastery was designed from the outset with intensive meditation in mind. In addition to the main shrine hall and monks' quarters, the complex includes dedicated meditation cabins set apart from the main buildings for extended solitary retreats, giving the monastery a particularly serene atmosphere. Its curriculum balances philosophical study of the Nyingma school's major texts — including the Nine Vehicles and Dzogchen teachings — with a disciplined daily meditation schedule. Parbing Samten Choling has produced several recognised meditation masters over the decades, contributing significantly to the living transmission of the Nyingma contemplative tradition in South Sikkim.

name: Tekling Dzokchen Monastery
history: Tekling Dzokchen Monastery, founded in 1936 in the village of Tekling, South Sikkim, is one of the most spiritually distinguished monasteries in South Sikkim, bearing in its name the identity of the highest teaching of the Nyingma school — Dzokchen, the Great Perfection. The founding lama was a direct disciple of one of the great Dzogchen masters of the era, from whom he received the complete transmission of the Longchen Nyingthig — one of the most celebrated Dzogchen teaching cycles. The monastery's main shrine is centred around a statue of Samantabhadra, the primordial Buddha of the Dzogchen tradition, surrounded by representations of the entire Dzogchen lineage. The walls are covered with intricate mandalas and depictions of the peaceful and wrathful deities of the Bardo, serving as practical guides for practitioners engaged in the highest meditative practices. For nearly nine decades, Tekling Dzokchen Monastery has attracted monks, yogis, and practitioners who seek the deepest teachings of the Nyingma tradition.

name: Namtse Ahaley Dechen Choling Monastery
history: Namtse Ahaley Dechen Choling Monastery, founded in 1948 in Namtse Ahaley, South Sikkim, was established in the year India gained independence. The name 'Dechen Choling' means 'The Place of Great Bliss Dharma,' invoking the Buddhist pure land of Dewachen — the realm of perfect bliss of the Buddha Amitabha. The founding lama was a prolific teacher and ritual master with extensive training in Nyingma philosophy, ritual performance, and sacred arts, who established the monastery as a comprehensive centre where all aspects of Buddhist culture would be preserved. One of the monastery's most celebrated activities is its annual performance of the Palchen Dupa — a major Nyingma ritual cycle lasting several days, in which hundreds of monks gather to perform elaborate offerings and sacred dances dedicated to the assembly of peaceful and wrathful deities. This complete ritual cycle, preserved at Namtse Ahaley even as it has been abbreviated or lost at many other monasteries, makes this institution a vital guardian of Nyingma ritual heritage in South Sikkim.

name: Sorok Tamang Monastery
history: Sorok Tamang Monastery, established in 1962 in the village of Sorok, South Sikkim, holds a distinctive place among the monasteries of South Sikkim as it is specifically associated with the Tamang community — one of the major Tibeto-Burman ethnic groups of the Himalayan region with a particularly devout Buddhist heritage. Founded in the same year that China launched its first nuclear test, and three years after the Tibetan exodus of 1959, the monastery was established partly to provide an institutional home for the Tibetan Buddhist lineages brought by refugee lamas, preserving them within the Tamang community of Sorok. The monastery is especially renowned for its tradition of Tamang Buddhist music — a unique regional style blending Tibetan Buddhist liturgy with indigenous Tamang musical elements — considered one of its most irreplaceable cultural treasures. The monastery has also played a crucial role in maintaining the Tamang language, oral literature, and traditional crafts in South Sikkim alongside its regular Buddhist educational programme.

name: Suiram Risung Monastery
history: Suiram Risung Monastery, founded in 1995 in the village of Suiram, South Sikkim, was established during the post-liberalisation era of India through the collaborative vision of local Buddhist families who felt a strong need for a permanent monastic institution in their village. The name 'Risung' evokes the image of a rising peak, symbolising the monastery's aspiration to elevate the spiritual life of the Suiram community. Prior to 1995, the community had relied on visiting monks for religious ceremonies, but the establishment of the monastery provided a permanent home for monks and a regular programme of prayers, teachings, and festivals. The founding was made possible through local fundraising, contributions from the wider Sikkimese Buddhist community, and support from senior lamas. The monastery was designed and built according to traditional Tibetan Buddhist architectural principles — careful orientation of the shrine room, proper placement of sacred images, and the creation of a harmonious spatial environment for meditation. Despite its relative youth, Suiram Risung has established itself as an important spiritual centre in South Sikkim.

name: Namthang Nyima Choling Monastery
history: Namthang Nyima Choling Monastery, established in 1997 in Namthang, South Sikkim, is a modern Buddhist monastery whose name 'Nyima Choling' means 'The Place of the Sun's Dharma' — suggesting bright, illuminating clarity that dispels the darkness of ignorance. Founded during a period of Buddhist renaissance in Sikkim, the founding lama had trained in both traditional Tibetan Buddhist institutions and modern academic settings, giving him a unique perspective on making ancient Buddhist wisdom relevant to contemporary communities. His vision for Namthang Nyima Choling was a monastery serving not only as a traditional place of worship but as a centre for modern Buddhist education, interfaith dialogue, and social service. The main shrine hall features a contemporary interpretation of traditional Tibetan Buddhist mural painting, executed by artists trained in both classical techniques and modern conservation methods. The monastery offers Dharma classes in the Nepali and Sikkimese languages for lay practitioners, making Buddhist teachings broadly accessible. It has also been involved in environmental conservation activities, reflecting its founder's belief that compassion must extend to care for the natural world.

name: Gagyong Monastery
history: Gagyong Monastery, founded in 2005 in the village of Gagyong, South Sikkim, represents the continuing vitality of Buddhist religious culture in 21st century Sikkim. Established through sustained devotion and generosity of the Gagyong community, the monastery was championed by senior local Buddhists who worked tirelessly to raise funds, secure land, obtain permissions, and invite a senior Nyingma lama to oversee its establishment. The founding lama, a recognised master who had spent years in retreat before taking on this responsibility, guided the construction according to precise traditional specifications, with every aspect of the design guided by Buddhist principles and ritual requirements. The monastery's main shrine features a magnificent representation of Guru Padmasambhava 'with Consorts,' surrounded by elaborate offerings arranged for the consecration ceremony. Though young by historical standards, Gagyong Monastery has quickly established itself as an active spiritual centre, hosting regular festivals, meditation programmes, and community prayers that draw participants from across the surrounding area.

name: Rabong Monastery
history: Rabong Monastery, established in 2006, is the younger of two significant monastic institutions in Rabong, South Sikkim — the other being the older Rabong Kunphenling Tsechu Monastery — and represents a 21st century renewal of Buddhist monastic culture in the area. Founded through a community initiative driven by younger generations of Rabong's Buddhist families, the monastery was made possible through local fundraising, support from the Sikkim government's Department of Ecclesiastical Affairs, and donations from Buddhist organisations across Sikkim and beyond. The founding ceremony was attended by senior lamas and government officials, reflecting the institution's importance to both the local community and the broader Sikkimese Buddhist establishment. The main shrine hall features a central altar with a large statue of Shakyamuni Buddha flanked by his two chief disciples, with walls bearing newly commissioned murals painted in the classical Palpung Kagyu style by Nepali artists. The monastery has developed active programmes in Buddhist education for both monks and lay practitioners, including weekly Dharma classes and an annual summer youth programme.

name: Rabong Kunphenling Tsechu Monastery
history: Rabong Kunphenling Tsechu Monastery, founded in 1972 — just three years before Sikkim's integration into India — is one of the most important and beloved Nyingma monasteries in South Sikkim. Its name 'Kunphenling Tsechu' carries multiple meanings: 'Kunphenling' means 'The Place That Benefits All Beings,' while 'Tsechu' refers to the sacred tenth day of the Tibetan lunar calendar associated with Guru Padmasambhava, on which major Nyingma festivals are held. The monastery was established during a momentous period in Sikkimese history, when the kingdom was on the cusp of political transformation, and its founding was an affirmation of the Buddhist community's commitment to preserving their traditions through change. The annual Tsechu festival — with monks performing elaborate masked sacred dances depicting Guru Padmasambhava's subjugation of demonic forces — is one of the most celebrated events in South Sikkim's religious calendar, drawing hundreds of pilgrims. The monastery has maintained a strong monastic community for over five decades and remains a vital centre of Nyingma culture.

name: Yangang Changchub Tamu Monastery
history: Yangang Changchub Tamu Monastery, established in 1982 in the village of Yangang, South Sikkim, is a spiritually vibrant Nyingma monastery whose name 'Changchub Tamu' means 'The Gateway to Enlightenment.' Founded seven years after Sikkim's integration into India, the monastery emerged during a period of social and economic transformation when communities were working to ensure Buddhist traditions would flourish in the new political environment. The founding lama, having witnessed the destruction of Tibetan monasteries following the Chinese occupation, was deeply committed to establishing strong Nyingma centres in Sikkim. He worked closely with the senior monks of the older Yangang Gonpa Tashi Palding Gon to ensure the two Yangang monasteries would complement each other's activities. Yangang Changchub Tamu became particularly dedicated to training young monks in traditional monastic arts — sacred dance, ritual music, sand mandala creation, and thangka painting — producing graduates who serve as ritual specialists and community leaders across Sikkim. The monastery continues growing with expanded educational programmes to meet the needs of a new generation of practitioners.
